title: Valkyrie V240 LITE Review
content:
Introduction


A big thank you to Valkyrie for providing the review sample!

Valkyrie is a relative newcomer in the DIY PC space, with the company launching numerous products within the last year. It features a full range of all-in-one liquid coolers, air coolers, and fans, all available in black or white. Suffice to say, they are on a roll. Add in a generous warranty, and you have a company worth watching to see where they go from here.


Today's review will focus on Valkyrie's all new V240 LITE all-in-one liquid cooler. Taking what they learned from the previous design, which was an exceptional performer, Valkyrie now is offering another interesting option that I have high hopes for. To start it uses a unique magnetic pump cover that features ARGB LED lighting along with en exposed circuit board, giving it a unique appearance that will likely be love it or hate it. Beyond that they are using a new fan design that has supposedly been optimized to provide the best performance possible on this closed loop cooler's radiator. Before we put those claims to the test let's take a closer look at the Valkyrie V240 LITE itself.

Specifications
Manufacturer:Valkyrie
Model:V240 LITE
Socket Support:Intel: LGA1851, LGA 1700, LGA1200, LGA115X, LGA2011, LGA2011-3, LGA2066
AMD: AM5, AM4
CPU Block:Base Material: Copper
Pump:RPM: 800–2800
Radiator:Dimensions: 276 x 120 x 27 mm
Material: Aluminium
Fans:Model: Valkyrie B12
Dimensions: 120 x 120 x 25 mm
Fan Speed: 800–2150 RPM (max)
Fan Airflow: 81.68 CFM
Fan Air Pressure: 4.00 mm-H2O
Fan Noise: 30.5 dBA
Features:Visible circuit board in the pump top adds an innovative look with ARGB lighting
2800 RPM high-speed pump for maximum cooling
B12 fans engineered for excellent radiator performance
Reliable and durable with a 5-year warranty
Warranty:Five years
MSRP:$119.90

Page 2

Packaging


The external box for the Valkyrie V240 LITE is black and red with the front detailing the cooler's easy installation, ARGB lighting, high-performance pump, and five-year warranty along with its 250 W TDP rating. Meanwhile, the left side is a red panel with the product name, company website and a logo denoting ARGB lighting. As for the back it lists all the specifications from the pump and fan PWM RPM ranges, CFM rating, supported CPU sockets, and a mechanical drawing with dimensions, among other things.


Taking a look on the right side it's the same as the left but in black rather than red. Moving on to the top though I found a rope handle which, while useless in terms of practicality, still a brought a smile to my face as it shows Valkyrie continues to push the envelope when it comes to packaging and unboxing of their products. Finally, the bottom of the box just has the product name big and bold so it can't be missed.

Contents


The box itself is a bit sturdier than I typically see, but that has more to do with the foam used internally than the box itself. Speaking of foam Valkyrie has fully encased the V240 LITE, providing more protection for the product than I typically see and this definitely gives the product and overall unboxing a much more premium feel.


The fans come pre-installed on the radiator. However, Valkyrie does include extra fan screws for a potential push/pull setup along with a Velcro cable tie and three tubing clips. Obviously you also have all the necessary hardware for installation on AMD's AM4 and AM5 platforms as well as Intel's LGA115x, all the way through to LGA2066.

Common parts supplied for mounting include:
  • 16x Fan screws
  • 8x Radiator screws
  • 4x Intel standoffs
  • 4x Intel LGA2011 standoffs
  • 4x AMD bracket screws
  • 4x Nuts
  • 2x AMD brackets
  • 2x Universal brackets
  • 2x 120 mm fans
  • 1x Intel Backplate
  • 3x Tube clips
  • 1x Tube of thermal paste
  • 1x Velcro cable tie
  • 1x Pre-filled liquid cooler

Page 3

A Closer Look


When it comes to the radiator, Valkyrie sticks to a traditional design. You will not find fancy side plates or ornate decorations beyond the Valkyrie logo, instead you get a simple boxy aluminium radiator. The fittings are black, with nylon sleeving over the tubing which measures 395 mm in length. Regarding the radiator's FPI (fins-per-inch), it is also typical at 20-21, meaning it is consistent with many other AIOs, and requires fans with decent static pressure for optimal cooling, the new B12 fans will certainly get put to the test.


The pump block on the V240 LITE is round under that fancy square pump cover which happens to have built in ARGB lighting in the left corner with an Infinity mirror design along with a look at the PCB as a unique visual element which can be seen a bit better in the second image. As for the fittings Valkyrie is using the swivel variety, and they are slightly offset in height, giving them a very good range of motion. I am also happy to see the wiring exit the pump from the opposite side of the tubing since once the cooler is installed the wiring is pointed straight up making for less cable routing. As for the base it is your standard convex copper cold plate and while some machining marks are visible most of what you see here was caused by me scrubbing the thermal paste off.


The B12 fans from Valkyrie are daisy-chained and equipped with ARGB LEDs located in the hub. In terms of performance metrics, the fans have a PWM RPM range of 800–2150 with a max CFM of 81.68 and a static pressure of 4 mm-H2O. It is worth noting that the connectors on the fans are not your typical 4-pin headers with separate ARGB header. Instead, it's a singular connector, however, it does terminate into standard headers via the extension cable.

Page 4

Installation


When installing the cooler the first thing to do is to make sure the wiring harness is in the right position for cable management in your build. Meaning you may need to switch the fans around to better hide the cables. Next remove the pump cover to make the mounting process easier. With that taken care of remove the AMD motherboard's plastic brackets and install the ones provided with the Valkyrie V240 LITE.


Now install the mounting brackets for the pump onto the motherboard brackets and apply thermal paste. For testing I use Arctic MX-6, but for the photo here I used generic paste for checking TIM spread. With the prep work finished you can mount the pump block to the CPU making sure to alternate between the two screws for more even pressure.


Now install the radiator and fan assembly and plug everything in making sure to position the pump cover and connect it to the existing wire harness.

Overall, the installation is pretty straightforward on AMD and Intel with no issues to speak of. With the fans pre-installed and the mounting hardware being simple and easy to work with it is a very easy AIO to install regardless of platform.

Page 5

Finished Looks


When the cooler is installed and ARGB lighting is not enabled it has a fairly high contrast black and white design that will fit most builds. Furthermore, even with the lighting disabled the pump cover with the visible PCB and Valkyrie logo still looks nice when light is not directly being reflected from it. The design remains rather clean and the daisy-chained fans and tubing clips help in that regard.


As for memory and graphics card clearance, there were no issues on either platform.


The ARGB lighting looks good and is fairly uniform. The brightness is good and the infinity mirror element on the pump cover adds a nice visual flair to go with the visible PCB design helping set the V240 LITE apart from other offerings. While the individual ARGB LEDs are visible from a side angle straight on they provide plenty of illumination that is consistently even, with little individual visibility of each LED.

Page 6

AMD Test System


Intel Test System


Supporting Hardware



Page 7

Temperature Readings

In our new CPU cooler reviews, you'll find a chart (like the one below), depicting the CPU temperature across the entire TDP range. This range spans from idle temperatures to the thermal throttle point, providing a quick visual representation of how well a tested CPU cooler performs as the heat load increases. We provide two charts, one at a cooler's maximum RPM and one at a noise normalized 45 dBA at 15 cm. As for ambient temperatures we test at 20-21°C.

Testing Methodology

No matter which exact processor model you choose for your rig—entry-level, midrange or high-end, AMD or Intel—they all come with a TDP rating from the vendor. Unfortunately these ratings are not standardized and vary greatly between vendors, and even between product generations. The first novelty with our new cooler review test setup is that we'll be testing each cooler at several TDP levels on both platforms, to provide you with data not only how it performs with the most powerful CPU, but also with something that's used by the more-average gamer.

While all modern processors have mechanisms that report the CPU power consumption, these readings are hardly accurate, and they also depend on various tweaks applied by the motherboard vendor and the BIOS settings (Load-Line Calibration, or LLC, for example). Instead, we opted for a hardware measurement solution. This approach allows us to physically monitor the processor's power draw, ensuring a significantly higher degree of accuracy. By directly monitoring the CPU's power usage, and integrating this data into our measurement pipeline, together with fine-grained control of the load parameters, we can provide results based on hardware-confirmed TDPs rather than relying on software estimates.

On a fundamental level, the testing method used will identical for both AMD and Intel. There will no longer be set clocks, voltages, or testing of cooler performance at base and sustained boost clocks. It makes more sense to set a TDP target that is physically monitored during testing. This allows the processor to operate at a controlled power level, allowing us to do performance testing that not only shows how a cooler should typically perform on entry or mid-range processors but also how these coolers will behave in various workloads. The data this provides can be correlated with our processor reviews, which display per-load and average power draw in various tests, including gaming, thus giving users a better grasp on the level of cooling their system needs.

This new testing method, designed to benefit all users, allows for a more practical evaluation of coolers. When utilizing a tower cooler with a 150-watt rating in an overclocking test, failures may occur due to the overclocked CPU generating excessive heat, and it might be dismissed by many as a choice for their setup. However, this scenario does not provide an accurate assessment of the cooler's overall performance, because the cooler was never designed to handle such high heat loads. Consequently, assessing coolers across various TDP settings offers a clearer understanding of their potential. Most importantly, this approach empowers users to choose coolers that precisely meet their needs, avoiding the unnecessary selection of overkill solutions.



AMD Power Consumption Reference

ProcessorCoresPower
Gaming		Power
Applications		Power
Maximum
Ryzen 5 56006 / 1238 W40 W62 W
Ryzen 5 5600X6 / 1245 W42 W61 W
Ryzen 7 5700X8 / 1648 W45 W61 W
Ryzen 7 5800X8 / 1668 W79 W125 W
Ryzen 7 5800X3D8 / 1652 W59 W90 W
Ryzen 9 5900X12 / 2485 W90 W126 W
Ryzen 9 5950X16 / 3289 W87 W117 W
Ryzen 5 76006 / 1649 W50 W75 W
Ryzen 5 7600X6 / 1666 W69 W115 W
Ryzen 7 77008 / 1656 W54 W82 W
Ryzen 7 7700X8 / 1662 W78 W138 W
Ryzen 7 7800X3D8 / 1649 W79 W77 W
Ryzen 9 790012 / 2464 W56 W74 W
Ryzen 9 7900X12 / 2486 W111 W200 W
Ryzen 9 7950X16 / 3289 W128 W254 W
Ryzen 9 7950X3D16 / 3256 W79 W140 W

AMD Socket AM5 Max Heat Load

Below, you will find our new maximum heat load chart, which indicates the sustained wattage each cooler can handle during testing before experiencing thermal throttling. Essentially, the higher the wattage a cooler can manage, the better it performs in challenging conditions. While every processor and system is unique with many factors, it gives us a useful idea of what both Air and AIO coolers can do.



AMD Socket AM5 Set TDP Results

Our new review style replaces the base clock / overclocked results with charts at specific TDPs, which demonstrate cooling performance at a certain power level. The lower wattage results give us a better idea of how a CPU cooler performs with entry-level processors. On the other hand, the mid and high TDP tests show the cooling performance under a heavy load for mid-range and high-end AMD and Intel CPUs.



Page 8

Temperature Readings

Just like on the previous page, we're starting with the chart showing CPU temperature across the entire TDP range. Do note that on Intel the thermal throttle point is not 95 °C like on AMD, but 100 °C, and it can be manually increased in the BIOS to 115 °C. As for ambient temperatures we test at 20-21°C.

Testing Methodology

No matter which exact processor model you choose for your rig—entry-level, midrange or high-end, AMD or Intel—they all come with a TDP rating from the vendor. Unfortunately these ratings are not standardized and vary greatly between vendors, and even between product generations. By directly monitoring the CPU's power usage, and integrating this data into our measurement pipeline, together with fine-grained control of the load parameters, we can provide results based on hardware-confirmed TDPs rather than relying on software estimates.

On a fundamental level, the testing method used will identical for both AMD and Intel. There will no longer be set clocks, voltages, or testing of cooler performance at base and sustained boost clocks. It makes more sense to set a TDP target that is physically monitored during testing. This allows the processor to operate at a controlled power level, allowing us to do performance testing that not only shows how a cooler should typically perform on entry or mid-range processors but also how these coolers will behave in various workloads. The data this provides can be correlated with our processor reviews, which display per-load and average power draw in various tests, including gaming, thus giving users a better grasp on the level of cooling their system needs.

This new testing method, designed to benefit all users, allows for a more practical evaluation of coolers. When utilizing a tower cooler with a 150-watt rating in an overclocking test, failures may occur due to the overclocked CPU generating excessive heat, and it might be dismissed by many as a choice for their setup. However, this scenario does not provide an accurate assessment of the cooler's overall performance, because the cooler was never designed to handle such high heat loads. Consequently, assessing coolers across various TDP settings offers a clearer understanding of their potential. Most importantly, this approach empowers users to choose coolers that precisely meet their needs, avoiding the unnecessary selection of overkill solutions.



Intel Power Consumption Reference

Gaming		Power
Applications		Power
Maximum
Core i5-13400F6+4 / 1643 W44 W65 W
Core i5-13600K6+8 / 2074 W104 W187 W
Core i7-13700K8+8 / 2489 W133 W252 W
Core i9-13900K8+16 / 32114 W166 W283 W
Core i5-14600K6+8 / 2076 W93 W174 W
Core i7-14700K8+12 / 28132 W155 W279 W
Core i9-14900K8+16 / 32144 W170 W282 W

Intel Socket LGA1700 Max Heat Load

The maximum heat load chart indicates the sustained wattage each cooler can handle during testing before experiencing thermal throttling. Essentially, the higher the wattage a cooler can manage, the better it performs in challenging conditions. While every processor and system is unique with many factors, it gives us a useful idea of what both air and AIO coolers can do.



Intel Socket LGA1700 Set TDP Results

Our new review style replaces the base clock / overclocked results with charts at specific TDPs, which demonstrate cooling performance at a certain power level. The lower wattage results give us a better idea of how a CPU cooler performs with entry-level processors. On the other hand, the mid and high TDP tests show the cooling performance under a heavy load for mid-range and high-end AMD and Intel CPUs.



Page 9

Noise and RPM Readings

Fan noise and RPM charts remain unchanged from previous reviews. They showcase the overall noise output of each cooler along with the corresponding RPM reading, giving users a quick look at how loud each cooler is across the entire fan/pump PWM range.

Page 10

Performance Summary and Performance per Dollar

The Performance Summary and Performance per Dollar charts have also been revamped. They now include both Intel and AMD results at both max RPM and 45 dBA noise normalized results for a quick comprehensive performance profile across all settings.

Meanwhile, Performance per Dollar is the same conceptually, it takes into account not only the performance of each cooler, but also its pricing, to determine the overall bang for the buck.

Page 11

Value and Conclusion

  • The Valkyrie V240 LITE has a MSRP of $119.90. But can be found at retail for $109.99
  • Exceptional performance overall
  • Perfect memory and GPU clearance
  • Fans have a wide RPM range
  • Good build quality
  • Visually interesting design
  • Daisy-chained fans keep cable management easy
  • ARGB lighting is aesthetically well done
  • Better pure value options are available
  • Loud at max RPM
  • ARGB lighting and pump cover design won't appeal to everyone
Performance
In terms of raw cooling performance the Valkyrie V240 LITE does exceptionally well. While the Arctic Liquid Freezer III 240 does outperform it in the noise normalized tests on our AMD test platform, the situation is reversed in the max RPM tests, where Valkyrie holds a clear lead. Meanwhile, on the Intel side it is no contest, with the Valkyrie V240 LITE absolutely dominating all 240 mm all-in-one liquid coolers, even beating down some 360 mm offerings. Looking at the results in general the cooler is definitely better suited for Intel systems but with that said, it is by no means a bad option for AMD builds either. Regardless of which platform you're going with performance remains impressive, and I will tip my hat to Valkyrie for delivering much more than I expected.

Noise
Noise levels are a bit hit or miss for the Valkyrie V240 LITE, while the wide RPM range means users have a good deal of control over the pump and fans it, doesn't change the fact the cooler does get loud at max RPM. Furthermore, hitting our 45 dBA noise normalized output target required the fans to be dropped all the way down to 56% on the PWM curve. While this means maximum performance will require dealing with much higher noise levels, at least you can tame them. Still, considering the overall cooling performance I believe the cooler would have been better served with a PWM range on the fans being 500-600 at the low end with the max being 2000. This would not impact performance much but would result in a better overall noise profile.

Build Quality
In terms of build quality I have no real complaints just potential nit-picking instead. The fit and finish is great, meanwhile the daisy-chained fans, magnetic pump cover, and offset swivel fittings are all great features that are well implemented. Sure, fan to fan direct connection would be cleaner than the cables but would likely result in much higher prices, so the trade-off is more than reasonable. Looking at the mounting hardware it was easy to use and simple with no problems to speak of on my review sample, and I am always a fan of great warranties which Valkyrie delivers with its five-years of support. If I had to nitpick something that irks me it would be the stickers on the fans. While spinning, you won't notice, but the logo is enough, the Valkyrie name repeating across it adds nothing to the visual design or aesthetics and from a first look aspect seems a bit tacky.

Other Thoughts
Beyond that I am glad to see the cooler comes encased in foam and is well protected from potential shipping damage on top of the inclusion of a proper well documented manual with clear images. Furthermore, while ARGB lighting is a love it or hate it feature Valkyrie's implementation here is very good and if you're not a fan the cooler doesn't look bad without it either. However, the exposed PCB visual element may be more of a hit or miss aspect here. Its slightly hidden and not an in your face element of the design, but it will definitely come down to personal taste.

Overall the Valkyrie V240 LITE is a great option if you need a 240 mm all-in-one liquid cooler. It performs similarly to some competing 360 mm options while having a good feature set, exceptional warranty and depending on your tastes a unique visual aesthetic. I have no problem recommending it especially if you're rocking an Intel based system.

Page 12

You are seeing this because the administrator of this website has set up Anubis to protect the server against the scourge of AI companies aggressively scraping websites. This can and does cause downtime for the websites, which makes their resources inaccessible for everyone.

Anubis is a compromise. Anubis uses a Proof-of-Work scheme in the vein of Hashcash, a proposed proof-of-work scheme for reducing email spam. The idea is that at individual scales the additional load is ignorable, but at mass scraper levels it adds up and makes scraping much more expensive.

Ultimately, this is a placeholder solution so that more time can be spent on fingerprinting and identifying headless browsers (EG: via how they do font rendering) so that the challenge proof of work page doesn't need to be presented to users that are much more likely to be legitimate.

Please note that Anubis requires the use of modern JavaScript features that plugins like JShelter will disable. Please disable JShelter or other such plugins for this domain.
author: crazyeyesreaper
